coredata.dc:title: Estimating nitroxynil residues in food samples and surface water using two different sustainable chemical sensing techniques: Comparative greenness, whiteness, and blueness study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yosrey, Eman
Elmansi, Heba
Shalan, Shereen
Nasr, Jenny Jeehan
coredata.dc:description: The presence of veterinary drug residues in edible animal tissues endangers human health and safety, accordingly, global nations enacted legislative restrictions to monitor their concentrations in food. In this work, we introduce two fluorimetric sensors for determining nitroxynil (NXL), a crucial veterinary medication, in various matrices. Sensor I, novel nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs), was fabricated from strawberry and leek juices as cheap and sustainable natural resources. Only five minutes of microwave radiation were required as a rapid synthesizing technique without any drastic conditions or organic solvents. The S,N-CQDs manifest blue emission using λex/em of 340/418 nm. The synthesized nanosensor was characterized using UV Spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and Fourier-transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR). Sensor II was the acriflavine reagent (AV) that exhibits native fluorescence using λex/em of 265/505 nm. Both sensors displayed quantitative turning off in their native fluorescence by increasing concentrations of NXL (1.0–10.0 and 0.5–8.0 µg mL−1) with correlation coefficients r = 0.9998 and 0.9999 for sensors I and II, respectively. The two probes achieved satisfactory percentage recoveries and standard deviation (SD < 4) for NXL assessing in complex matrices of diverse food samples (bovine muscle, kidney, and fat) and Fasciolar® veterinary formulation. Additionally, they have been successfully used to detect the investigated drug in river ecosystems with a mean %recovery of 100.10 ± 1.70, and 99.95 ± 1.31 for sensors I and II, respectively. The two constructed sensors’ specificity has been confirmed for all tested matrices reflecting their feasibility as promising platforms in therapeutic drug monitoring, livestock ecosystems, food safety assurance, quality control of drugs, and environmental pollution monitoring. Quenching mechanisms for both methodologies were studied and the reaction stoichiometry for NXL − AV complex was calculated in two ways. Lastly, a comprehensive comparison was performed between the suggested methodologies and published fluorimetric works. The greenness feature was assessed by the Analytical Eco-scale (AES), Green Analytical Procedure Index (GAPI), and Analytical GREEnness Metric Approach (AGREE). In addition, white analytical chemistry principles were checked to assess sustainability criteria using the RGB 12 algorithm. The recently launched Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI) metric tool was also employed to assess the practicality (Blueness) character.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitroxynil
Quantum dots
Acriflavine
Sustainable
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reaction stoichiometry between NXL and AV

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is funded by Mansoura University (Project code number: Mu-phar-23-33).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, (b) FTIR of the synthesized S,N-CQDs.
(a) Attributes of S,N-CQDs at different pH (b) Excitation/emission spectra of S,N-CQDs, UV-absorption spectra of CQDs and NXL. (c) Attributes of S,N-CQDs using λex 300–400 nm.
Effects of: (a) different pH, (b) different volumes of CQDs on the quenching of S,N-CQDs by NXL.
(a) Excitation/emission spectra of AV and UV-absorption spectra of NXL. (b) Effects of different volumes of AV on the quenching of AV by NXL.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of: (a) Blank S,N-CQDs, (b) The remainder of S,N-CQDs after the reaction with NXL (4.0 μg mL−1) using λex 340 nm. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of: (a) Blank AV (1.6 × 10-4 M) in BRB (pH 8), (b) The remainder of AV (1.6 × 10-4 M) after the reaction with NXL (5.0 μg mL−1) in BRB (pH 8) using λex 265 nm.
Mechanism of the ion association complex between AV and NXL.
Performance data of NXL by the proposed methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: −Sa = standard deviation of the intercept of regression line.
−Sb = standard deviation of the slope of regression line.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed and official methods for determining NXL in its raw material.

bold.underline: Statistical comparison between the two suggested methods

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values between parentheses are the tabulated t and F values respectively, at p = 0.05 (67).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed and the official method for determining NXL in the veterinary dosage form.

bold.underline: Statistical comparison between the two suggested methods

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values between parentheses are the tabulated t and F values respectively, at p = 0.05 (67).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed methods for determining NXL in food samples and surface water.

head.ce:title: Estimating nitroxynil residues in food samples and surface water using two different sustainable chemical sensing techniques: Comparative greenness, whiteness, and blueness study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura National University, Gamasa 7731168, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura National University, Gamasa 7731168, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The presence of veterinary drug residues in edible animal tissues endangers human health and safety, accordingly, global nations enacted legislative restrictions to monitor their concentrations in food. In this work, we introduce two fluorimetric sensors for determining nitroxynil (NXL), a crucial veterinary medication, in various matrices. Sensor I, novel nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S,N-CQDs), was fabricated from strawberry and leek juices as cheap and sustainable natural resources. Only five minutes of microwave radiation were required as a rapid synthesizing technique without any drastic conditions or organic solvents. The S,N-CQDs manifest blue emission using λex/em of 340/418 nm. The synthesized nanosensor was characterized using UV Spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and Fourier-transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR). Sensor II was the acriflavine reagent (AV) that exhibits native fluorescence using λex/em of 265/505 nm. Both sensors displayed quantitative turning off in their native fluorescence by increasing concentrations of NXL (1.0–10.0 and 0.5–8.0 µg mL−1) with correlation coefficients r = 0.9998 and 0.9999 for sensors I and II, respectively. The two probes achieved satisfactory percentage recoveries and standard deviation (SD < 4) for NXL assessing in complex matrices of diverse food samples (bovine muscle, kidney, and fat) and Fasciolar® veterinary formulation. Additionally, they have been successfully used to detect the investigated drug in river ecosystems with a mean %recovery of 100.10 ± 1.70, and 99.95 ± 1.31 for sensors I and II, respectively. The two constructed sensors’ specificity has been confirmed for all tested matrices reflecting their feasibility as promising platforms in therapeutic drug monitoring, livestock ecosystems, food safety assurance, quality control of drugs, and environmental pollution monitoring. Quenching mechanisms for both methodologies were studied and the reaction stoichiometry for NXL − AV complex was calculated in two ways. Lastly, a comprehensive comparison was performed between the suggested methodologies and published fluorimetric works. The greenness feature was assessed by the Analytical Eco-scale (AES), Green Analytical Procedure Index (GAPI), and Analytical GREEnness Metric Approach (AGREE). In addition, white analytical chemistry principles were checked to assess sustainability criteria using the RGB 12 algorithm. The recently launched Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI) metric tool was also employed to assess the practicality (Blueness) character.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Veterinary drugs are broadly used in livestock systems to either treat, prevent diseases, or improve production efficiency. The probable presence and accumulation of these drug residues in edible animal tissues above certain limits may jeopardize the health of consumers. This is due to the incidence of allergic reactions, developing bacterial resistance and other toxic problems [1–3]. Consequently, assuring food safety through regular food control is an important issue that arouses countries’ concerns. At the global level, the Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO) [4], the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [5] and the European Union (EU) [6] set up restrictive legislations to definite maximum residual limit (MRL) for each drug. Hence, proposing analytical methodologies for determining drug residues in different food tissues has become a pressing need [7,8]. For the analysts, suggesting specific assaying methods in the presence of a complex food matrix (protein, fat, carbohydrates) with simple pre-preparation steps and low detection limits is challenging.

The inappropriate disposal of antibiotics is a prime source of surface-water contamination. Some antibiotic residues can cause potential hazards to human health, aquatic life, and phytosystems even in concentrations of ng/L [9]. Besides, excessive exposure to these antibiotics can lead to the emergence of antimicrobial resistance, which may be difficult to overcome. Therefore, there is a growing need to propose analytical methodologies that allow the sensitive and facile detection of these emerging pollutants in different water samples [10–12].

An important veterinary medication, Nitroxynil (NXL; 4-Hydroxy-3-iodo-5-nitrobenzonitrile, Fig. 1
a) is a halogenated phenol administered subcutaneously for the treatment of fasciola in sheep and cattle [13]. Fasciolosis is a liver fluke disease that occurs in sheep and cattle by Fasciola hepatica and Fasciola gigantica. These flukes adversely affect animals’ milk productivity and fertility, and also pave injury with mastitis and pregnancy toxaemia [14–16]. This causes a burden in livestock farming and financial loss for farmers, butchers, and countries. In the fluke’s mitochondria, NXL ceases ATP formation through uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation [17,18].

So far, different analytical techniques have been reported for quantifying NXL in different matrices, these include HPLC [19–25], voltammetry [26,27], polarography [28] and using immunosensor [29]. Investigating the practical procedures for the previous methods revealed that they mainly depended on using hazardous solvents or harsh chemicals and costly techniques that need to be manipulated by a skilled operator. It is worth noting that only two fluorimetric methods have been discussed to assess NXL [30,31]. Despite their ability to detect very low concentrations, they used non-green solvents (conc. HCl, DMSO, hexane, dichloromethane) or costly materials (albumin), and required 15 min for reaction completion or many steps for sensor synthesis. The limited fluorimetric publications for NXL are attributed to the deprivation of fluorophores or commonly derivatized groups like amino groups [32]. Herein, we developed two facile, rapid ecofriendly probes for the sensitive assessment of NXL. Microwave-synthesized S,N carbon quantum dots and acriflavine fluorescent sensors were suggested and validated for determining NXL in raw materials and various matrices.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a quasi-zero dimensional carbon nanomaterial, has gained widespread attention in recent years as a rising paradigm in nanotechnology. Thanks to its fascinating properties especially cytotoxicity, favorable biocompatibility, outstanding water solubility, tunable emissions, and ease of doping [33,34], it has been used in chemical sensing [35], biomedical [36] and environmental analysis [37] disciplines.

Two primordial models have been suggested for synthesizing CQDs: top-down and bottom-up models. In top-down methods, the carbon material (e.g., graphite, carbon nanotubes) is exfoliated into CQDs through either electrochemical oxidation, or chemical oxidation using a strong acid, laser ablation, and arc discharge [38]. On the contrary, bottom-up models where biomass resources (e.g., fruits, and vegetables) are fused and reconstructed by hydrothermal /solvothermal, microwave pyrolysis, combustion, and electrochemical techniques [39,40]. From the green chemistry viewpoint, the latter models are preferred where CQDs can be synthesized in bulk from renewable resources [41]. In this context, microwave pyrolysis is utilized as a facile energy input source where waves penetrate the material yielding heat within a few minutes [42]. Employing microwave techniques offered supreme features of cleanness, and rapidity with low-consumed energy compared to other bottom-up techniques with long-time procedures and non-green solvents and drastic chemicals. Only five minutes of microwaves (700 w) were used to irradiate strawberry (Fragaria × ananassa) juice as carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen source [43,44] in combination with leek (Allium ampeloprasum var. porrum) juice as sulfur and nitrogen source [45] for fabrication of self-doping S,N-CQDs. The constructed S,N-CQDs exhibited excellent water solubility, satisfactory stability, and blue fluorescence properties authorizing their utility for assessing pharmaceuticals.

Acriflavine reagent (AV) was exploited as another fluorescent probe for assessing NXL residues in raw materials and different matrices. Acriflavine (Fig. 1 b) is an acridine basic dye first discussed by Paul Ehrlich in 1912. It is chemically named 3,6-diamino-10-methylacridin-10-ium chloride with a molecular weight of 259.74 [46]. It possesses antiseptic, anticancer, antiviral, antimalarial, antimicrobial and, interestingly, anti-SARS-CoV-2 properties [47–49]. On the other side, it has fluorescent properties that make it suitable to use to determine many pharmaceutical and essential compounds [50–53].

ce:list-item.ce:para: A Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA), equipped with a xenon flash lamp employed at the sensitivity of 800 and 485 V for sensors I and II, respectively. The slit width was fixed at 5 nm and a smoothing factor of 20 was selected.
A Centrifuge (Sigma model 2-16P, Germany) and vortex mixer (VM-300P, Germany) were utilized throughout the work.
The pH was adjusted using a pH meter (Jenway model 3510, United Kingdom).
A Smart MH8265CIS microwave (LG Electronics, Korea) has been used to synthesize CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para:
The utilized chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade:
•
Nitroxynil (B.N.1120018) was kindly supplied by Natco LabChemical ReagentsCo. Cairo, Egypt, with 100.12 % purity as certified.
•
Acriflavine reagent (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) was prepared as an aqueous solution 8.0 × 10−4 M which was further diluted to 1.6 × 10−4 M for performing the study.
•
Britton Robinson Buffer (BRB) was prepared using 0.04 M for each acetic acid, boric acid, phosphoric acid, and adjusted using 0.2 M sodium hydroxide (El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co., Abu-Zabaal, Cairo, Egypt).
•
Organic solvents (methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile) products of Sigma-Aldrich, Germany.
•
Fasciolar® parenteral vial (NXL 25 %) (B.N. 1185/22) manufactured by the Arab company for medical products, 6th October, Giza, Egypt.
•
Food samples were purchased from a local market.
•
Surface water sample was collected from El-Mansouria Canal, Mansoura, Egypt

Fresh strawberries and leeks were purchased from local markets. White bulb of leeks was removed and washed well with tap water then distilled water. They were chopped using a domestic chopper and squeezed using a muslin cloth to obtain their juices. This was also performed for strawberries, they were firstly washed, ground, and squeezed to get their juices. Using a glass beaker, (15:5, v/v) leek and strawberry juices were irradiated in the microwave at 700 w for 5 min. They were removed from the microwave and allowed to cool at room temperature. The formed residue was rinsed with 20 mL of distilled water and sonicated for 30 min. After that, it was centrifuged at 4800 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was then filtered using a 0.45 µm membrane syringe filter. The obtained brown solution was stored in an amber glass bottle in the refrigerator at 4 °C. The preparation was kept stable for three months indicated by the stability recordings in fluorescence intensity. For performing the methodology, a working solution of S,N-CQDs was prepared by allocating 10 mL of the obtained preparation to a 100 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with distilled water.

An accurately weighed amount (0.01 gm) of NXL was transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask to prepare a standard stock solution at a concentration of 100.0 µg mL−1 using ethanol [13] as a solvent. A working ethanolic solution (50.0 µg mL−1) was prepared from which appropriate dilutions were prepared to cover the studied concentration range. Standard solutions were protected from light and maintained in the refrigerator for maximum stability [13].

To assess NXL, increasing volumes from NXL working solution were transferred to 10 mL volumetric flasks to give a final concentration range (1.0–10.0) µg mL−1 followed by 300 µL of working solution of S,N-CQDs. The mixtures were well mixed and made up to the volume using distilled water as a diluting solvent. The fluorescence (F) was recorded at 418 nm upon excitation at 340 nm at room temperature (25 °C). The fluorescence intensity of a blank experiment (F°) was recorded following the same steps without adding the drug. ΔF value was calculated for each concentration (ΔF = F°-F) and plotted against the corresponding NXL concentration in µg mL−1 to construct the calibration curve and derive the regression equation.

Analysis of NXL was performed by relocating aliquots of NXL working solution to a set of 10 mL volumetric flasks, followed by 0.5 mL 0.04 M BRB (pH 8) and 1.3 mL of AV (1.6 × 10-4 M). The flasks were well mixed and completed to the mark with distilled water giving a final concentration (0.5–8.0) µg mL−1. The fluorescence measurements (F) were recorded instantaneously at 505 nm using λex at 265 nm. The procedure was performed by omitting the drug to record blank reading (F°). ΔF was calculated (ΔF = F°-F) and plotted versus NXL concentration in µg mL−1 to compute the corresponding regression equation.

An aliquot from Fasciolar® parenteral vial (NXL 25 %) equivalent to 10 mg was transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask and completed to the mark with ethanol. The flask was sonicated for 15 min to effectuate complete solubility to obtain a homogenous solution. Working standard solutions through the studied concentration range were prepared to check the concentration of NXL. The procedures under section 2.5.1. and 2.5.2. were followed, and the corresponding regression equations were utilized to calculate % recovery and standard deviation.

Five grams of each grounded bovine muscle, kidney and fat were accurately weighed and spiked separately with aliquots of NXL in centrifuge tubes. These tubes were vortexed for 2 min to allow penetration of NXL to tested tissues. To obtain a uniform homogenate, 10 mL of methanol was added to each tube and placed in an ultrasonic bath for 15 min. The tubes were then centrifuged at 4800 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 µm syringe filter and 1.0 mL from the filtrate was transferred to a 50 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the final volume with distilled water to obtain a concentration of 100.0 µg mL−1. Serial dilutions were performed within 10 mL volumetric flasks to obtain the studied concentration in the calibration curves for the two methods. These concentrations were assayed following the proposed methods and percentage recoveries were calculated.

The surface water sample was collected from El-Mansouria Canal, Mansoura, Egypt in (1 L) clean plastic bottle prerinsed with distilled water. The collected water sample was kept aside to allow the settling down of the suspended particles and then filtered through filter paper. Within 10 mL volumetric flasks, water samples were spiked with aliquots of NXL and tested following the suggested methodologies and the percentage recoveries were computed.

Cheap and widely available fruit and vegetable were chosen as sustainable sources to synthesize nano-sensor to analyze NXL residues in different matrices. The synthesis process depended on using the microwave-assisted pyrolysis of strawberry and leek juices within only 5 min. Strawberry was exploited as a carbon and nitrogen source, while leek was used as a sulfur and nitrogen source. We utilized the contactless heat transfer of MW as an inexpensive and quick technique to obtain nanoparticles. Microwaves cause alteration in the magnetic and electric fields of the material, modulating molecular heat levels. This generates energy in much shorter times compared to conventional techniques [42].

At first, the perception of yellow/brown color in daylight and blue color fluorescence upon subjecting to a UV lamp (Fig. SI) gave a plausible prediction for producing carbon dots [54]. We started to study the optimum synthesizing conditions. The impact of the leek and strawberry juices ratio was studied at (20:0, 15:5, 10:10: 5:15 and 0:20, v/v, respectively) while keeping the irradiation time constant. The results revealed that the highest fluorescence intensity was recorded by using leek: strawberry juices (15:5, v/v), respectively using (340/418 nm) as λex and λem (Fig. S2
a). This volumetric ratio was then employed to study the microwave processing time. Different processing times were investigated (3, 4, 5, 6 and 7 min). No charring was observed below 3 min processing time; high fluorescence intensity was recorded by increasing the irradiation time to 5 min which was chosen to be the optimum processing time (Fig. S2 b). This boosting in the fluorescence intensity by increasing the irradiation time could be attributed to the generation of more CQDs. It was observed that the over-irradiation time lessens the fluorescence intensity, which was explained by the turn-off phenomenon as a result of damaging the CQDs surface, and these results came in agreement with the reported literature [55,56].

The morphology and crystal size of CQDs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The image (Fig. 2 a) showed quasi-spherical particles with size 10.14 ± 2.99 nm. Functional groups of N,S-CQDs were investigated using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Fig. 2 b showed a broad peak at 3426 cm−1 which is attributed to vibrational stretching of N–H and O–H, the peak for C–H appeared at 2929 cm−1. The bending vibration for CC, CO, or CN appeared as a strong narrow peak at 1632 cm−1. The absorption peak at 1403 cm−1 indicated the presence of SO and C-N, while the peaks at 1055 cm−1 and 655 cm−1 referred to SO, C-O and S-S, respectively [57]. These manifestations were attributed to dehydration which led to carbonization, besides functionalization through doping from strawberry juice ingredients (amino acids, protein, carbohydrates) [43,44] and leek juice ingredients (organosulfur components, phenolic, flavonoid and protein) [58,59].

Attributes of the synthesized S,N-CQDs in different pH values (2–12) [2 –12] were studied using BRB. The fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs was nearly constant in acidic and neutral medium, and a substantial decrease was observed in alkaline pH (Fig. 3
a). By scrutinizing the literature review, it was found that different attributes with suitable explanations were reported [60]. Wang’s group explained the decrease in the fluorescence intensity as pH increases by aggregation of the particles due to non-covalent molecular interactions formed between the functional groups on the S,N-CQDs surface [61]. Another explanation was discussed by Zhao et al., who interpreted the high fluorescence intensity by the rigidity of CQDs due to the H-bond formed between functional groups on the S,N-CQDs surface. This H-bond was decreased in the alkaline medium due to deprotonation of the functional groups leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity [62].

Upon recording the UV spectrum of the synthesized CQDs (Fig. 3 b), two absorption peaks at 210 and 285 nm have been discerned. The peak at 210 nm referred to π – π* of aromatic sp2 domains, while the latter peak at 285 nm is attributable to n – π* of N or O transitions [63]. Additionally, noting an extended tail to the visible region hinted a low-energy CQDs surface attributed to its functionalization [63]. Illuminating CQDs solution with a UV lamp at 365 nm showed bright blue color forecasting fluorescence properties ( Fig. S1 ). The fluorescence emission was then recorded using excitation wavelengths range (300–400) nm with 10 increments. It was noticed from Fig. 3 c that an up trending in the emission intensity reached maxima at λex 340 nm then gradual down-conversion was observed. Also, a broad emission peak was obtained at a lower excitation wavelength that gets narrower as λex increased, this was accompanied by red shifting in the emission wavelength. These findings came in agreement with those reported in the literature [64,65].
Various analytical criteria were studied to obtain the best sensitivity for NXL determination, these include different pH values, CQDs reagent volume and incubation time.

Different pH values [2–12] using 0.04 M BRB were tested and ΔF values were calculated (Fig. 4
a). A decrease in ΔF values was observed in alkaline pH [8–12] and no significant enhancement was obtained when compared with no buffer usage. As a result, the methodology was conducted without any buffer.

Increasing CQDs reagent volumes were studied to attain the highest sensitivity. As displayed in Fig. 4 b, maximum and steady results were observed between (200–400 µL), so 300 µL was selected as a suitable volume to perform the method.

Different times for the incubation between the S,N-CQDs and NXL were screened. ΔF value was recorded; instantaneously, 1, 5, 10, 20 and 30 min after the addition of the drug. The experiments showed virtually constant results. So, all measurements were performed instantaneously, adding quick and simple measurement merits to the method.

Constructing sensor II based on the rigidity of AV structure that gives inherent fluorescence at 505 nm using λex 265 nm (Fig. 5
a). The increasing aliquots of NXL lead to a quantitative drop in AV fluorescence intensity. Factors affecting the interaction of NXL with AV were inspected and optimized to obtain the best sensitivity as follows:

In chemical reactions, a buffering system is favoured to obtain the optimum ionization form of the reactants and to keep the pH constant throughout the work, hence different pH values (2–12) [2 –12] using 0.04 M BRB were investigated. Constant and maximum results were obtained by using pH values (3–9) [3 –9] and no enhancement was noticed compared with no buffer. Searching the reported analytical methods showed specificity and stability of AV at a slightly alkaline medium, so pH 8 was utilized to conduct the proposed method. Different volumes of pH 8 were then studied and 0.5 mL was used as a suitable volume.

To select the optimum AV volume, a study was performed through the range (0.2–2.0) mL at 0.2 increments using (1.6 × 10-4 M) AV. The results were augmented up to 1.2–1.4 mL after which the recordings were diminished (Fig. 5 b). The low responses at the low AV volumes were related to the incomplete reaction, on the contrary, the diminishing at the higher volumes was attributed to the aggregation of AV molecules leading to self-quenching. Consequently, 1.3 mL was selected as the optimum volume for the method.

Water, methanol, ethanol, and acetonitrile were tested as diluting solvents to select the maximum quenching results. Turning-off fluorescence was topmost using water and slightly increased when methanol was tested, but water (the best green solvent) was selected due to its economic and eco-friendly features. The observed decrease in fluorescence results with other tested organic solvents may be due to the denaturation of the formed complex with organic solvents.
Validation of the proposed methodologies was performed in compliance with the International Conference on Harmonization (ICH) guidelines [66] as follows:

Linearity was tested by plotting ΔF values against NXL concentrations in the ranges (1.0–10.0) and (0.5–8.0) µg mL−1 for sensors I and II, respectively. Table 1 points out excellent linearity with correlation coefficient (r) close to unity for both sensors. Very low concentrations reached up to 0.16 and 0.50 µg mL−1 for sensor I, and 0.11 for 0.32 µg mL−1 for sensor II could be detected and quantified, respectively. These concentrations were calculated from the equations; limit of detection (LOD) = 3.3 Sa/ slope, and limit of quantification (LOQ) = 10 Sa/ slope, where Sa is the standard deviation of intercept. The calculated LOQ values for both sensors were lower than the established MRL of NXL in the European Commission and CodexAlimentariusCommission which were (400.0 µg kg−1) in bovine muscle, kidney and (200.0 µg kg−1) in fat. To ascertain repeatability and intermediate precision, three different concentration levels were examined three times within the same day and on three consecutive days, respectively. Results abridged in Table S1 reflect acceptable SD, %RSD and %Error proving precise character for the suggested works. The aspect of accuracy could be investigated by comparing the results of the two developed methods with the official pharmacopoeia method [13] using Student’s t- and Variance ratio F- tests. The official pharmacopoeia method includes UV measurement of NXL at 271 nm in alkaline solution. The calculated values of Student’s t- and Variance ratio F- tests in Table 2 didn’t exceed the tabulated values, this manifested the accurate property for both proposed methods [67]. The specificity of both sensors was proved by achieving acceptable percentage recoveries for NXL in the presence of surrounding matrix components. Results collected in Tables 3 and 4
revealed the capabilities of assigned sensors to determine NXL in either dosage forms, complex edible tissue, or river water.

The addition of increasing concentrations of NXL to either S,N-CQDs or AV results in quantitative quenching in the fluorescence intensity of blank reagent (Fig. 6 ). Inspecting the UV spectrum of NXL (Fig. 3 b), it shows two considerable absorbance peaks at 270 and 405 nm. This overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of the S,N-CQDs (Fig. 3 b), and the excitation spectrum of AV (Fig. 5 a), which predicts the occurrence of quenching by the inner filter effect (IFE). IFE quenching occurs due to the absorption of the quencher to the excitation and/or emission energy [68]. To make certain of the suggested mechanism, the following equation was utilized to calculate the corrected fluorescence intensities:
F
c o r r e c t e d
=
F
o b s e r v e d
×
a n t i l o g
(
A e x
+
A e m
2
)
where Aex and Aem are the absorbance of NXL at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the studied fluorophore, respectively.

Suppression efficiency (%E) was then calculated for both the observed and corrected fluorescence intensities as follows:
% E
=
( 1 -
(
F
F
°
)
) ×
100
The results in Fig S3 revealed a massive turning-off of the fluorescence by IFE for both S,N-CQDs and AV up to 78.50 and 76.0 %, respectively by NXL aliquots. This means the contribution of other mechanisms in quenching of both fluorophores by the drug.

Secondly, Stern-Volmer plots were utilized to investigate the temperature impact on the formed complex. Experiments were conducted at three different temperatures and F°/F were plotted against molar concentrations of NXL.
F ° / F = 1 +
K
sv
NXL
where Ksv is the quenching constant and [NXL] is the molar concentration of the drug.

In Fig. S4 , linear straight lines were obtained with a correlation coefficient close to unity for both S,N-CQDs and AV, this excluded the occurrence of combined static and dynamic quenching, which is characterized by a nonlinear relationship with upper curvature. Ksv values were then computed to deduce whether it is a static or dynamic quenching mechanism. In static quenching, a non-fluorescent complex is formed when the fluorophore in the ground state (before excitation) interacts with the quencher. It could be identified from a decrease in Ksv values because of increasing temperature. The contrary is dynamic quenching, whose Ksv values were increased as the temperature increased. Dynamic “or collisional” quenching is formed due to complex formation between the quencher and fluorophore in the excited state [69]. From results abridged in Tables S2 − S3, Ksv values were temperature-dependent for both fluorophores. In sensor I, Ksv values were decreased by raising the temperature denoting static quenching, while in sensor II, Ksv values were increased as the temperature elevated indicating the dynamic mechanism. The findings reveal massive counting of quenching for both suggested fluorophores on IFE and static or dynamic quenching for sensors I, and II to a lesser extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction stoichiometry between NXL and AV
ce:section.ce:para: In Job’s study, equimolar concentrations from both NXL and AV were prepared. The diverse molar ratios from the two solutions were prepared with a constant total molar ratio. The responses noted in Fig. S6 showed that the results present an uptrend reaching the maximum at a 1:1 ratio.
In the formed complex, the nitro group in NXL is easily ionized and interacts with the quaternary amino group of AV resulting in an ion association complex outlined in Scheme 1 .

Using the corresponding regression equations, the two devised sensors have been utilized to quantify NXL in Fasciolar® parenteral vial. Excellent percentage recoveries were attained with SD values of ±1.19 and ±1.41 for sensors I and II, respectively. From the results abridged in (Table 3), the specificity of the sensors was ascertained and the interference from excipients was neglected. Moreover, these results were compared statistically with the results of the official spectrophotometric method [13] using Student’s t- and Variance ratio F- tests and no significant difference was detected assuring the methods’ accuracy and precision [67].

Thanks to the highly sensitive feature of the submitted sensors with LOQ levels lower than official MRL values (400.0 µg kg−1 for muscle and kidney, 200.0 µg kg−1 for fat), they have been used to assess NXL in bovine muscle, kidney, and fat tissues. The spiked samples of NXL were analyzed using the two aforementioned procedures and the calibration equation was derived for each tissue. The findings (Table 4) showed satisfactory recoveries with good SD values for all experimented matrices by sensors I and II. These results revealed the absence of interference from the complex protein, lipid, and other matrix constituents. Also, the results accentuate the high specific property of the two sensors and can be assigned as simple, green, and facile platforms in food quality assurance.

Effluents from farms, hospitals or antibiotic manufacturers are usually discharged into surface water including rivers. Accordingly, testing the specificity of the designed probes for monitoring NXL in river water was environmentally essential. The spiked water samples were tested using the suggested procedures and calibration curves were constructed for each methodology. The values summarized in Table 4 reveal acceptable percentage recoveries and SD for both methods. These results affirmed the absence of interference from the possible present waste residues in the surrounding matrix. Also, reflect the importance of the suggested sensors for environmental monitoring.

Reducing the consumption of organic solvents and directing toward renewable safe reagents is the core of green analytical chemistry (GAC) principles [72]. Different metric tools have been reported for the assessment of greenness property. A comprehensive comparison has been carried out between the suggested works and the reported fluorimetric methods. To obtain a satisfactory estimation for the suggested frameworks, three tools have been used namely, Analytical Eco-scale (AES), Green Analytical Procedure Index (GAPI) and Analytical GREEnness Metric Approach (AGREE). Firstly a quantitative evaluation was performed using AES [73]. It depends on allocating penalty points for parameters opposing GAC. It evaluates the consumed reagents, instrument energy and produced waste. These points are then subtracted from 100. As illustrated in Table S4
, the suggested procedure achieved 90 and 86 proving excellent green methodologies compared with the reported spectrofluorimetric methodologies [30,31] that achieved 81 and 77, respectively. Additionally, GAPI software has been exploited as a qualitative and quantitative tool to evaluate the greenness of the assigned procedures. It demonstrates the estimation through five pentagrams with 15 sections that attain either green, yellow, or red colors reflecting the good, medium, and bad impact on the environment. As presented in Table S5
, a higher number of green sections in our works are fulfilled compared with the reported methods reflecting superiority in greenness character. In the case of the food samples application, an extraction procedure is required where section 5 will take red color in the chart of the methods. Additionally, the AGREE metric tool has been utilized to estimate the greenness property [74]. In AGREE tool, the twelve principles of GAC were evaluated through colored evaluation, green, yellow, and red, assigned for excellent, medium, and bad environmental impact, respectively. Also, a numerical score is given to each criterion and the overall score is listed in the middle of a clock-shaped pictogram. The results in Table S5 assure higher scores with a higher number of green sections for the suggested procedures over the reported methods. The findings collected in Tables S4-S5 reflect the superiority of the greenness merit for the proposed methods compared with the published ones.

Delving deeper into sustainability aspects provoked analysts to introduce white analytical chemistry with 12 principles [75]. These principles have evolved as an extension of green analytical chemistry principles to strike a balance between the method’s analytical performance and eco-friendly features. These criteria are evaluated using RGB 12 algorithm through a free, available Excel worksheet with three colored tables (red, green, and blue) to illustrate validation, greenness attributes, and practical feasibility, respectively. The outcome is expressed with an average numerical score (the best achieves 100) and a merged color (the best displays white). In this context, we made good use of this recently discussed tool to generate an inclusive comparison between the suggested methods and the reported spectrofluorimetric methods. Table S6 denotes the superiority of the reported methods [30,31] regarding sensitivity (LOD and LOQ). However, the proposed sensors outperformed the published works in the scope of analysis, as they were applied to dosage forms, food matrices, and water analysis, compared with the reported method [30] that was applied to dosage forms, food matrices, or method [31] that was applied for food matrices only. Additionally, the greenness attributes were applied more strongly for the suggested methods achieving 93 % and 93.3 % for sensors I, and II, respectively. Regarding the practical criterion, sensor II achieved the best score (88.8 %), followed by the reported spectrofluorimetric procedure [30] with a score of 87.5 % and sensor I got a score of 87.1 %, whereas, the published procedure [31] with a score of 78.1 % came in last. Overall, the results of the adopted methodologies elucidate that they are compiled more strongly with the whiteness principles, making them profitable for multiple significant applications.

For a more manifold and comprehensible evaluation, the Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI) has been employed as a novel metric tool for comparing the suggested methodologies with reported works. BAGI has been recently designed for the assessment of the practicality (Blueness) of the analytical methodologies [76]. This tool estimates the practicality through the evaluation of ten attributes. It is discussed as aligned with the principles of environmental sustainability and complementary to the greenness metric tools. The evaluation outcome is presented through an asteroid pictogram with blue grade shading (dark blue, blue, light blue, and white) denoting high-, moderate-, low-, and non-agreement with the set criteria, respectively. It also gives a numerical score for each section, with an overall score and average color represented in the middle of the pictogram. The displayed pictograms in Table S7 show that the applicability of sensor II achieved the highest score “62.5”, followed by “60” assigned for sensor I and the reported spectrofluorimetric method [30], while the reported spectrofluorimetric method [31] achieved the lowest score “55”.

In this work, we have designed a novel lab-made nanosensor from natural feedstock to record NXL in various matrices. Self-S and N doping was achieved using strawberry and leek juices through microwave carbonization. Additionally, AV was utilized as a fluorescent sensor for NXL determination in a facile manner. The two proposed platforms depended on quenching of probes’ fluorescence by the drug and the validation criteria were excellently achieved. Interestingly, the suggested sensors could specifically determine NXL in complex matrices of bovine muscle, kidney, fat, veterinary dosage formulations and river water giving acceptable percentage recoveries. The methods outperformed other reported methodologies in simplicity, greenness, whiteness, and blueness. These merits were checked using AES, GAPI, AGREE, RGB 12, and BAGI metric tools. In a nutshell, the two proposed sensors could successfully be employed to assay NXL in food quality assurance, quality control of drugs and environmental drug monitoring.
This article does not include any studies with human participants or animals carried out by any of the authors
Informed consent is not applicable in this study.
This research is funded by Mansoura University (Project code number: Mu-phar-23-33).

Eman Yosrey: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Heba Elmansi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Shereen Shalan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Jenny Jeehan Nasr: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2024.125146.

ce:section.ce:para:
